sound_type: powerful liquid gobble -- rapid rolling vocalization of ~30 pulses per second, audible at 1+ mile, one of the loudest bird vocalizations in North America; E=6 maximum energy
ecosystem: Oak woodland, mixed forest, forest edges, riparian corridors. Reintroduced to PNW -- not native to most of Washington. Eastern Washington oak-prairie and ponderosa pine habitats. Expanding range.
season: Year-round resident. Gobbling peaks March-May (breeding season). Dawn gobbling from roost trees. Shock gobble response year-round.
family: Phasianidae (1st entry. FIRST PHASIANIDAE. The gamebird family enters the engine.)

coordinates.description: Wild Turkeys established in eastern Washington oak-pine habitats -- Klickitat, Yakima, Okanogan Counties. Introduced populations expanding. Some western Washington foothills populations.

taxonomy_note.description: FIRST Phasianidae entry. Gamebird family enters the engine. Reintroduced to PNW -- not native. Three subspecies introduced: Eastern, Rio Grande, Merriam's. National recovery from ~30,000 (1930s) to 7+ million. Conservation triumph. E=6 maximum energy returns after 45-degree gap.
taxonomy_note.family_count_in_engine: Phasianidae: first entry. Wild Turkey (153) begins the Phasianidae quartet (153-156).
taxonomy_note.name_etymology: Meleagris from Greek 'meleagris' (guinea fowl, later applied to turkeys through European naming confusion). gallopavo from Latin 'gallus' (rooster) + 'pavo' (peacock) -- rooster-peacock, describing the male's ornate display.
taxonomy_note.genus_note: Meleagris -- first entry. Only two species in genus: Wild Turkey (M. gallopavo) and Ocellated Turkey (M. ocellata). Endemic to the Americas.
taxonomy_note.order_note: Galliformes. First entry in this order. Gamebirds -- turkeys, grouse, pheasants, quail, partridges.
taxonomy_note.family_position: First Phasianidae. Opens the gamebird quartet (153-156). E=6 maximum. The loudest gamebird in North America.

song_metrics.fundamental_frequency_range_hz: Gobble: 100-9000 Hz broad spectrum. Most energy in 1000-4000 Hz band.
song_metrics.peak_energy_band_hz: 1000-4000 Hz. Low-frequency components (100-500 Hz) aid long-distance propagation.
song_metrics.song_frequency_hz: Gobble: 100-9000 Hz. Broad spectral range for a single vocalization.
song_metrics.song_duration_s: Individual gobble: ~1 second. Gobbling bout: multiple gobbles at 5-30 second intervals, continuing minutes to hours.
song_metrics.note_structure: GOBBLE: ~30 rapid pulses per second creating continuous liquid rolling quality. Syringeal production with crop/throat sac resonance amplification.
song_metrics.note_types: Gobble (primary, breeding/territorial), yelp (hen, communication), cluck (attention), purr (contentment), kee-kee run (lost call), alarm putt (danger).
song_metrics.phrase_structure: Individual gobble: ~1 second, ~30 pulses. Bouts: repeated gobbles at 5-30s intervals. Dawn chorus: multiple toms chain-reaction gobbling.
song_metrics.timbre: Liquid, rolling, resonant, powerful. The rapid pulse rate creates a tremulous quality unique among bird vocalizations. Instantly recognizable.
song_metrics.amplitude: E=6. 100+ dB at close range. Audible at 1+ mile under favorable conditions. One of the loudest bird vocalizations in North America.
song_metrics.tempo: ~30 pulses/second within gobble. Gobbling bouts: 5-30 second intervals. Dawn chorus: continuous from multiple toms.
song_metrics.context: Breeding season (March-May peak). Dawn from roost tree. Response to rival males, hens, loud environmental sounds (shock gobble).
song_metrics.regional_variation: Some variation between subspecies (Eastern, Rio Grande, Merriam's). Individual variation in pitch and cadence.
song_metrics.key_property: GOBBLE -- 100-9000 Hz, ~30 pulses/second, liquid rolling, 100+ dB, audible 1+ mile. E=6 MAXIMUM. FIRST PHASIANIDAE. Returns E=6 after 45-degree gap. Reintroduced to PNW. National recovery 30K to 7M+. Ben Franklin's bird.

plumage.overall: Males: dark, iridescent bronze-green overall. Bare head and neck with red, white, and blue coloration (changes rapidly during display). Long breast beard. Tail fans into semicircle during strut. Females: duller, smaller, brown-toned.
plumage.diagnostic_mark: Males: iridescent bronze body, bare colored head/neck, breast beard, leg spurs. Females: brown, smaller. Both: large ground bird.
plumage.seasonal_variation: Head/neck coloration intensifies during breeding. Beard and spurs grow with age. Otherwise minimal seasonal change.
plumage.flight_pattern: Strong burst flight up to 55 mph. Short distance. Explosive takeoff. Roost in trees nightly.

physical_description.overall: Length: 100-125 cm (males), 76-95 cm (females). Weight: 5-11 kg (males), 2.5-5.4 kg (females). Among the heaviest flying birds in North America.
physical_description.diagnostic_mark: Large ground bird. Males: iridescent body, bare head, breast beard, tail fan in display. Leg spurs. Females: smaller, browner.
physical_description.field_id: Unmistakable. Very large ground bird in open woodland. Males display with fanned tail, puffed body, wing drag. Listen for the gobble -- audible at extreme distance.

nodes.title: Gobble -- Liquid Thunder
nodes.description: ~30 pulses/second creating liquid rolling quality. 100-9000 Hz broad spectrum. Crop/throat sac resonance amplification. 100+ dB. Audible 1+ mile. Amplitude modulation aids long-distance propagation. One of the loudest bird vocalizations in North America.

nodes.title: First Phasianidae -- Gamebirds Enter
nodes.description: Phasianidae enters the engine at degree 153. Gamebird family: turkeys, grouse, pheasants, quail. Ground-dwelling. Extraordinary acoustic diversity. Quartet: Turkey (153), Dusky Grouse (154), Ruffed Grouse (155), Sooty Grouse (156).

nodes.title: E=6 Returns After 45-Degree Gap
nodes.description: Last E=6 at degree 108 (Pileated Woodpecker laughing). 45 degrees without maximum energy. Wild Turkey gobble breaks the drought. Longest gap between E=6 events in the engine.

nodes.title: PNW Restoration -- Not Native, Now Established
nodes.description: Wild Turkey not native to PNW. Introduced by WDFW: Eastern, Rio Grande, Merriam's subspecies. Eastern Washington oak-pine habitats. National recovery: 30,000 (1930s) to 7+ million. One of the great conservation success stories.

cross_references.relationship: E=6 PAIR! Last E=6 on SPS axis (108). Wild Turkey restores E=6 after 45-degree gap. Both maximum-energy vocalizations.
PHASIANIDAE PAIR! Turkey gobble (153, E=6, loudest) to Dusky Grouse booming (154, E=5, subsonic). Loudest to lowest frequency in the family.
PHASIANIDAE MECHANICAL! Turkey gobble is vocal. Ruffed Grouse drumming is mechanical. Same family, different sound production. Fourth mechanical sound in engine.
FAMILY TRANSITION! Columbidae (149-152) complete. Phasianidae (153-156) begin. Doves to gamebirds. Urban to woodland.
